word_list.word: the harder you work, the luckier you get
word_list.pronunciation: ðə hˈɑːɹdɚ juː wˈɜːk ðə lˈʌkiɚ juː ɡˈɛt

senses.definition: used to emphasize the value of hard work, suggesting that success is often the result of persistent effort, rather than luck or chance

word_list.word: if you build it, they will come
word_list.pronunciation: ɪf juː bˈɪld ɪt ðeɪ wɪl kˈʌm

senses.definition: used to imply that if one invests time, effort, and resources into creating something worthwhile, others will recognize its value and be naturally drawn to it
used to emphasize the value of hard work, suggesting that rewards or benefits are often earned through effort and contribution
used to emphasize that in order to achieve something valuable or desirable, one must be willing to put in the necessary effort and hard work

word_list.word: revolutions are not made with rose water
word_list.pronunciation: ɹˌɛvəlˈuːʃənz ɑːɹ nˌɑːt mˌeɪd wɪð ɹˈoʊz wˈɔːɾɚ

senses.definition: used to emphasize that significant changes are not achieved easily or without sacrifice and struggle

examples.x: The workers went on strike because they knew that revolutions are not made with rose water, and they had to fight for their rights.

word_list.word: Rome was not built in a day
word_list.pronunciation: ɹˈoʊm wʌz nˌɑːt bˈɪlt ɪn ɐ dˈeɪ

senses.definition: used to emphasize that significant achievements or accomplishments require time, effort, and patience, and cannot be completed quickly or easily

examples.x: Don’t worry if the project seems overwhelming; Rome was not built in a day.
She had to remind her team that success doesn’t come overnight; Rome was not built in a day.
Building a strong team takes time, as Rome was not built in a day.
Learning to play an instrument takes practice, and as they say, Rome was not built in a day.
She reminded herself that learning a new language takes time—Rome was not built in a day.

word_list.word: you (do not|never) get something for nothing
word_list.pronunciation: juː duːnˌɑːt nˈɛvɚ ɡɛt sˈʌmθɪŋ fɔːɹ nˈʌθɪŋ

senses.definition: used to suggest that everything has a cost or a price, and that achieving something requires effort, investment, or contribution

examples.x: Even though he wanted to get in shape, he knew that you don't get something for nothing and that he had to put in the effort to exercise and eat healthy.

word_list.word: elbow grease is (always|) the best polish
word_list.pronunciation: ˈɛlboʊ ɡɹˈiːs ɪz ðə bˈɛst pˈɑːlɪʃ

senses.definition: used to imply that the best results are achieved by hard work

examples.x: The gardener knew that keeping a garden beautiful required a lot of physical labor and effort, since elbow grease is the best polish when it comes to maintaining plants and flowers.

word_list.word: genius is one percent inspiration, (and|) ninety nine percent perspiration
word_list.pronunciation: dʒˈiːnɪəs ɪz wˈʌn pɚsˈɛnt ˌɪnspɚɹˈeɪʃən ænd ɔːɹ nˈaɪnti nˈaɪn pɚsˈɛnt pɚspɚɹˈeɪʃən

senses.definition: used to imply that success or great achievements are mostly the result of hard work, dedication, and effort, rather than simply being a matter of natural talent or inspiration

word_list.word: God makes the back to the burden
word_list.pronunciation: ɡˈɑːd mˌeɪks ðə bˈæk tə ðə bˈɜːdən

senses.definition: used to imply that every individual has the capability to overcome their own unique challenges, despite how overwhelming they may appear, by relying on their inner strength and resilience

examples.x: The soldier knew that he had to be strong and resilient, because God makes the back to the burden, and that he had the inner strength to face the challenges of war.

word_list.word: the longest way (round|around) is the nearest way home
word_list.pronunciation: ðə lˈɑːŋɡəst wˈeɪ ɹˈaʊnd ɔːɹ ɐɹˈaʊnd ɪz ðə nˈɪɹəst wˈeɪ hˈoʊm

senses.definition: used to caution against the temptation of choosing shortcuts or quick fixes that may appear easier or faster but could lead to undesirable consequences in the long run

examples.x: The doctor advised his patient to follow the recommended treatment plan, saying that the longest way round is the nearest way home and that taking shortcuts could lead to more health complications in the future.

word_list.word: money does not grow on trees

senses.definition: used to imply that money is not easily obtained or available, and one should not waste it unnecessarily

examples.x: My parents always reminded me that money does not grow on trees, encouraging me to save and spend wisely.
We need to stick to our budget because money does not grow on trees and we can't afford to be reckless with our finances.
The manager emphasized to the employees that money does not grow on trees, so cost-cutting measures were necessary.
Whenever the kids asked for expensive toys, their father would say, "Remember, money does not grow on trees."
She turned down the luxurious vacation proposal, reminding her partner that money does not grow on trees and they needed to be practical.

senses.definition: used to suggest that one should put in maximum effort or take a bold approach to achieve success, or else it is better not to try at all

examples.x: You have to be willing to put everything on the line if you want to succeed; it's go big or go home, as they say.

word_list.word: he who would catch fish must not mind getting wet
word_list.pronunciation: hiː hˌuː wʊd kˈætʃ fˈɪʃ mˈʌst nˌɑːt mˈaɪnd ɡˌɛɾɪŋ wˈɛt

senses.definition: used to imply that success requires effort and perseverance, and that people must be willing to face obstacles and discomfort to achieve their goals

examples.x: The athlete knew that if he wanted to win the championship, he would have to train hard every day, even in adverse weather conditions, understanding that he who would catch fish must not mind getting wet.
